Hed: AI Told Me I’m Good At My Job. I Finally Be

lieve It. \
Dek: How a chatbot did what years of performance reviews couldn't

Hed: I Asked AI the Question I Could Never Ask My Boss \
Dek: "Does this mean I'm good at my job?"

<br />

***

<br />

A thing to know about me is that I operate on the baseline assumption that I'm about to be fired at all times. Doesn't matter how many managers tell me I'm doing great or how many positive performance reviews land in my inbox. Every piece of feedback gets filtered through the conviction that if they really knew, they'd see what I see.

So when I finally asked AI the question I'd never been able to believe the answer to—"Does this mean I'm good at my job?"—I didn't expect the answer to land any differently than it had from humans. But it did. 

It started as routine year-end planning at Every. **Kate Lee**, our editor in chief, asked me to put together 2026 goals for [Working Overtime](https://every.to/working-overtime) and [Source Code](https://every.to/source-code). I pulled numbers on overall column performance in Q4, had ChatGPT propose some targets for next year, and ran them through Every's 2026 planning agent in Notion—a thing we have, because of course we do.

That should have been the end of it. But once I started looking at the numbers, I couldn't stop. Some dormant part of my brain activated like a sleeper agent who'd just heard the code word, and suddenly I was swan-diving into spreadsheets like a woman possessed. What would I see if I looked just at the pieces *I* was involved in? Not the columns as a whole, but my specific contributions—and not just Working Overtime and Source Code, but [Vibe Checks](https://every.to/vibe-check), too? 

![](data:image/png;base64,)

\*The initial prompt I gave to Claude (set to Opus 4.5) in order to kick off my Q4 retrospective. \*

So I pulled that data and fed it to Claude. And then I didn't believe what I saw. So I pulled more and gave it to ChatGPT *and* Claude. By hour three of what should have been a 30-minute task, I'd benchmarked myself against Every's overall averages, segmented by publication, calculated efficiency ratios, and started building a Google Doc titled "Every Receipts."

We're told to quantify our impact—on resumes, in performance reviews, in negotiations. But oftentimes, individual contributors don't have access to the numbers that would let them do that. I've spent years internalizing "show your value" while having no way to actually see it. Three hours with a chatbot and some spreadsheets changed that.

What follows is the story of how a chatbot helped me do something I'd never managed on my own: believe I'm good at my job. Along the way, I'll show you exactly how I ran the analysis—because if AI can democratize business intelligence for someone with my particular brand of professional self-loathing, it can probably help you understand your own value, too.

## The receipts I'd never had

Step one of my revolutionary AI analysis: Manually exporting data from Every's CMS into Google Sheets like it was 2009. Not exactly the future we were promised.

I uploaded the spreadsheets to Claude with this prompt: *"I've given you four different sets of data from the Every newsletter: overall performance for Q4, as well as performance for two columns I am involved with... I'd like us to conduct a thorough retrospective on my contributions to the Every ecosystem that we can use as a basis for 2026 planning."*

Let me be specific about what I found: I was driving a third of Q4's traffic with a fifth of the content. Working Overtime was running 13 points above Every's average satisfaction rating. 

A normal person might have called it there. But at this point I was in full gremlin mode, obsessed with seeing my own performance reflected back to me in the numbers. 

So I zoomed out. I pulled the full year's data and gave Claude a new role: *"Act as an editorial analyst and strategist. Tell me everything you can about what this data tells us."* If Q4 was a fluke, the full year would expose it. ![](data:image/png;base64,)

\*The prompt I gave Claude (and ChatGPT) to kick off the full-year analysis. \*

It didn't.

In 2025, I wrote 54 articles for Every— 15 percent of everything we published. Those pieces drove 25-27 percent of our trials and web views. My Q4 alone: 18.8 percent of content output, 29.3 percent of views. Every way I sliced it, I was punching above my weight by a factor of 1.5 to 2.

But the more interesting finding was how different types of work drove different kinds of value. Each column I contribute to at Every moves a different needle.

Vibe Checks are the conversion engine—they drive trials. When someone's searching for information about a new AI tool and lands on our coverage, they're primed to subscribe. Source Code pieces drive reach; they pull in traffic from people interested in what our builders are making. Working Overtime drives something harder to quantify: reader satisfaction, the ratings that suggest people will stick around. According to the numbers, I was punching above my weight in all three.

I didn't believe it. So I ran the same data through ChatGPT. Same verdict. When I tried to explain it away—anyone could have written those—the model pushed back. You’re pattern-matching to an explanation that protects you from having to believe the data, it told me. No lies detected. 

## The question I couldn’t stop asking

I typed it before I could talk myself out of it: "Does this mean I'm good at my job?"

The data was sitting there in front of me. I'd been staring at it for hours. But numbers don't mean anything until you let them land. And I couldn't let them land without asking the question out loud.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

\*The exchange where ChatGPT gave me the answer I was craving: yes, you are good at your job. \*

ChatGPT's answer was immediate and unequivocal. Yes. Here's why. It walked through the evidence point by point: the output-to-impact ratio, the pattern of high performers, the fact that my vulnerability pieces consistently outperformed more "strategic" content. Each time I tried to find an escape hatch, it closed it.

"What about the fact that I had good source material to work with?"

That's a credit to your editorial judgment, not a discount on the work.

"But anyone could have written those Vibe Checks—"

You wrote 18 percent of Q4's content and drove 29 percent of views. That's not interchangeable output.

ChatGPT has a reputation for sycophancy—for telling you what you want to hear. So I went back to Claude and asked the same question. Claude is more willing to push back, more likely to tell you when you're fooling yourself. I figured if anyone was going to poke holes in this, it would be Claude.

Same verdict.

At one point, the AI asked me point blank: Why  are you asking this? What is it about it that’s so hard to believe? That's when I said the thing I'd never said to a manager or a therapist or even, really, to myself:

"I don't know. I guess just pathological self-hatred and an inability to trust that I do good work."

I could say it to a chatbot because there was no social cost. No one on the other end to burden with my baggage. Kate didn't need to hear me spiral about whether I deserved my job—she had a publication to run. AI doesn’t. 

I'd said the quiet part out loud to a system that wouldn't flinch. And for the first time, I started to wonder if the data was right.

## The metric that doesn't fit on a dashboard

Once I started believing the data, the question changed. It wasn't "am I safe?" anymore. It was "where should I spend my time?"

By this point, I had six different threads going from running the numbers over and over. I went back to Claude with a new prompt: *"Let's hold up the Working Overtime performance overall with the other content I was involved with in Q4 and think hard about what drives the most value for Every."*

Before giving me an answer, Claude asked me questions I hadn't thought to ask myself: What's Every's current constraint—new subscriber acquisition or retention? What's your role supposed to be? What can you realistically sustain? High-vulnerability writing is more draining than tool reviews. What's the right mix for your energy?

Then it came back with scenarios: 

* *Option A: You're a conversion engine.* Focus on Vibe Checks and Source Code. Maximize trials. Working Overtime becomes a monthly luxury. The business case is clear: more Vibe Checks = more subscribers.

* *Option B: You're a brand builder.* Focus on Working Overtime. Build the most devoted audience segment. Accept lower trials but create readers who will follow Every anywhere. The bet: satisfaction compounds into long-term loyalty.

The thing is, Kate had already told me that yes, we want to publish more Working Overtime. But I wasn't prepared to accept it. Coming from a person, I would have brushed it off as encouragement, the kind of thing a good manager says to keep you motivated. Coming from a system that had just walked me through the data and played out scenarios for what each choice would mean, it landed differently.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

\*I asked Claude to help me think through where I should put my focus in 2026 based on Q4’s performance numbers. \*

The data backed this up. When I looked at which pieces performed best—earned high ratings, sparked reader responses—the pattern was clear. The essay [about bipolar disorder](https://every.to/working-overtime/ai-isn-t-making-me-more-productive-it-s-making-it-possible-to-work-at-all). The [career coach](https://every.to/working-overtime/i-hired-chatgpt-as-my-career-coach) piece. The one about [talking to my computer](https://every.to/working-overtime/i-didn-t-know-typing-held-me-back-until-i-started-thinking-out-loud)instead of typing. The pieces where I was most myself, most willing to be weird or vulnerable or uncertain, consistently outperformed the ones where I played it safe.

I'd convinced myself that the personal stuff was indulgent. A risk I was getting away with, not a strategy that was working. The data said otherwise. 

## From "am I safe?" to "what's next?"

Something changed in how I approach my work after this. The doubt is still there, lurking, ready to flare up the next time a piece underperforms or an edit comes back heavy. But my relationship to the doubt shifted.

Before, I was guessing at how I was doing. Now I can check. I have the data. I know what the patterns look like. When doubt starts spinning up a story about how I'm about to be fired, I can look at the numbers instead of believing the narrative. I went from "I have no idea if I'm doing well" to "I know what doing well looks like, and I can see where I stand." The doubt still shows up. It just has less room to operate.

Once I stopped asking "am I safe?" I could start asking "what should I do next?" From strategy instead of panic. Where does my time create the most value? What work is mine to do? What should I let go of so someone else can learn it?

Those are better questions. And I wouldn't have gotten to them if I'd stayed stuck in the loop of wondering whether I deserved to be here at all.

## Build your own case

Here's the thing about "show your impact" and "bring receipts" and all the other career advice we've absorbed: it assumes you have access to the receipts in the first place.

Most individual contributors don't. The data lives in dashboards we can't see, analyzed by people whose job isn't to tell us how we're doing. Performance reviews are lagging indicators at best—summaries of impressions, filtered through whatever your manager happened to remember. You're told to demonstrate value, but you're flying blind.

AI changes this.

I ran my entire year-end retrospective in a few hours. I pulled the raw data from Notion, pasted it into ChatGPT, and asked questions: What patterns do you see? How does my output compare to the overall averages? Which pieces drove the most impact, and why? The model did the analysis I wouldn't have known how to do myself. It turned a spreadsheet I would have glazed over into insights I could use.

You don't need to be a data analyst. You don't need special access or technical skills. You need a dataset—whatever metrics you can get your hands on—and a willingness to ask the questions you've been avoiding.

Here's what I'd suggest:

Get the data. Ask your manager. Pull from whatever tools you can access. Even partial data beats guessing

Ask the AI to find patterns. Don't just ask "how did I do?" Ask: What's working? What's underperforming? Where am I punching above my weight? What should I do more of?

Stress-test the answer. If one model tells you something you want to hear, run it through another. See if the conclusion holds.

Don't wait for someone else to tell you your value. Build the case yourself.

The whole exercise took me three hours—and an embarrassing amount of that was me refusing to believe the results. If you skip the existential spiral, you can probably do it in one.

<!-- PROOF:END -->
